main_summary.summary: Elbert "Big Man" Howard, a co-founder of the Black Panther Party, was interviewed in Santa Rosa, California on June 30, 2016 by David Klein for the Civil Rights History Project. Born in 1938 in Tennessee and raised by his mother and aunts in segregated schools, Howard joined the Air Force in 1956, served four years including a posting in Europe, and discharged into Oakland. While studying at Merritt College on the GI Bill he met Bobby Seale and Huey Newton, helped develop the Ten Point Platform and Program, and became the founding editor of The Black Panther newspaper. He recounts the Denzil Dowell case in Richmond, the mimeographed first issue, securing printing through Max Scherr at the Berkeley Barb, Emory Douglas's artwork, and Richard Aoki's Asian-community organizing. Howard later served as deputy minister of information, traveled to Japan, Sweden, Denmark, and Germany on Party business, and worked on legal defense for jailed Panthers, including the Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins New Haven trial and the Attica prison negotiations. He describes Sam Napier's murder in New York, his own jailing in the Tombs, leaving the Party after Huey Newton dissolved the chapters, and later co-founding PACH in Sonoma County.
key_themes: Founding of the Black Panther Party
The Black Panther newspaper and Party media
Police accountability and armed community patrols
Legal defense and political prisoner support
main_summary.historical_significance:
Howard is one of the six original founding members of the Black Panther Party and the founding editor of its newspaper, giving this interview unusual authority on the Party's Oakland origin period (1966-1967), the development of the Ten Point Platform, and the build-out of the newspaper from a single mimeographed sheet on the Denzil Dowell case to a weekly with reported print runs of 250,000. The interview is also a primary-source account of the Party's international outreach (Japan, Sweden, Denmark, Germany) and of specific high-profile defense episodes — the New Haven trial of Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins, the Attica negotiations, and the Sam Napier killing during the East Coast-West Coast schism.

Howard's testimony about COINTELPRO tactics, including FBI agents pressuring his elderly aunt and the Freedom of Information Act file he later received, complements documentary records of federal surveillance of the Party. His account of the Merritt College parolee work-study program and the late-1970s decision to leave the organization following Huey Newton's directive to consolidate chapters in Oakland adds first-person framing to the Party's institutional decline. The closing material on his Sonoma County police-accountability work (PACH) and the Andy Lopez shooting links the Panther organizing model directly to ongoing 21st-century activism.

chapters.title: Tennessee Childhood and Segregated Schools
chapters.summary: Howard, born in Tennessee in 1938, describes growing up without his father, raised by his mother and aunts. He attended segregated schools through the 11th grade and recalls the 1954 conversation about integration in his community, saying he had his own circle of friends and was indifferent to integrating white schools.

chapters.title: Air Force Service and Europe
chapters.summary: Howard enlisted in the Air Force in 1956 after the Korean War, choosing it over the Army on advice from returning veterans. Stationed in Europe, he visited Paris, Stockholm, and Copenhagen, and encountered jazz musicians who had relocated for artistic freedom. He observed that European whites differed sharply from the segregationists of the South.
related_events: Black expatriate jazz scene in Europe

chapters.title: Oakland, Merritt College, Meeting Newton and Seale
chapters.summary: Howard discharged in Northern California, settled in Oakland, and enrolled in Merritt College on the GI Bill. He began studying Black history for the first time and met Bobby Seale and Huey Newton, with whom he held rap sessions on police repression in Oakland. The group sought a non-civil-rights, revolutionary alternative and began drafting the Ten Point Platform.
related_events: Founding of the Black Panther Party (1966)
Drafting of the Ten Point Platform and Program

chapters.title: Revolutionary Influences and Police Confrontation
chapters.summary: Howard describes the Party's study of Che Guevara, Mao Zedong, Kwame Nkrumah, and Malcolm X, while insisting the Party would not emulate Cuba or China. He recounts a personal incident at Don Barksdale's club in Oakland — a selective police ticket and subsequent arrest — that pushed him to commit to the BPP, including buying a legal 12-gauge shotgun after consulting Newton on the law.

chapters.title: Police Patrols and the Denzil Dowell Case
chapters.summary: The Party began armed police patrols in Oakland, observing arrests and following members through bail. Howard recounts the Richmond shooting of Denzil Dowell, whose family told the Party Dowell could not have jumped a fence as police claimed. The case triggered the Party's first organized rally and its first mimeographed publication — the seed of the Black Panther newspaper.
related_events: BPP armed police patrols in Oakland
Denzil Dowell shooting and Richmond Courthouse demonstration

chapters.title: Editing The Black Panther Newspaper
chapters.summary: Howard became the founding editor of the Party newspaper at Seale's request. When Eldridge Cleaver was released from prison after publishing Soul on Ice, Cleaver took the editor title and Howard became Deputy Minister of Information. He describes the paper's role in countering the Oakland Tribune and San Francisco Chronicle and building a weekly circulation that reached 250,000 copies.
related_events: Founding of The Black Panther newspaper
Eldridge Cleaver's release from prison and BPP entry

chapters.title: Producing and Distributing the Paper
chapters.summary: Howard describes the difficulty of getting printers because the FBI pressured them, and how Max Scherr of the Berkeley Barb offered his presses. Emory Douglas produced the artwork. He describes the Party newspaper's national distribution by chapter members, federal interference with airline shipping, and the paper's eventual international reach into Asia, Japan, and African nations.
related_events: BPP newspaper printing through Berkeley Barb
International distribution of The Black Panther

chapters.title: Richard Aoki and Coalition Building
chapters.summary: Howard tells the Party assigned Richard Aoki to organize the Asian community, and Aoki worked with the Red Guard Party in Berkeley. He describes coalitions with the Young Lords (Puerto Rican), the American Indian Movement, and Latino organizations, and his rebuttal of later FBI-informant allegations against Aoki, with whom he remained a lifelong ally.
related_events: Posthumous FBI-informant allegations against Richard Aoki

chapters.title: Cross-Country Legal Defense Work
chapters.summary: After Cleaver took the editor's chair, Howard traveled across the country visiting jailed Party members, working with attorneys on legal defense, and organizing community defense committees. He describes survival programs — free breakfast, free clothing, free health screening for sickle-cell anemia, and liberation schools — as both community service and a strategy for sustaining support for incarcerated members.
related_events: BPP free health clinics and sickle-cell testing
Memphis Invaders becoming a BPP chapter

chapters.title: Japan and European Tour
chapters.summary: Howard and Roberta Alexander were sent to Japan in 1971 to speak at anti-Vietnam-War student rallies, working with filmmaker Shinsuke Ogawa and shadowed by Japanese surveillance. Howard continued to Sweden, Denmark, possibly Finland, and Germany, where he met with Black GIs on a base whose commander imposed a blackout to prevent their visit; they met instead in a nearby village.
related_events: BPP support committees in Sweden and Denmark

chapters.title: New Haven Trial and Attica
chapters.summary: Recalled from Europe when Bobby Seale was extradited to New Haven on the Alex Rackley murder charge, Howard worked on the Seale-Huggins defense for the duration of the 14-to-18-month trial, drawing support from Daniel Berrigan, Dr. Benjamin Spock, and Kingman Brewster. Shortly after, Howard accompanied Seale to Attica Correctional Facility during the 1971 uprising, where Governor Nelson Rockefeller refused to negotiate and the prison was retaken by force.
related_events: Kingman Brewster's statement on Black revolutionary trials

chapters.title: Sam Napier and the East-West Coast Schism
chapters.summary: Howard recounts the call from New York reporting Sam Napier's murder. Napier ran the BPP newspaper distribution. Howard arrived to find women and children sheltered in a hotel and was arrested with two comrades by NYPD, jailed in the Tombs, and represented by William Kunstler. The judge dismissed charges on condition Howard leave New York. Robert Webb had earlier been shot in retaliation amid the East Coast-West Coast schism inside the Party.
related_events: Sam Napier killing in New York (1971)
Robert Webb shooting in New York

chapters.title: Merritt College Parolee Program
chapters.summary: Returning to Oakland, Howard was hired by Merritt College's president to run the work-study program and negotiated with the California Adult Authority to enroll parolees in college, providing GED preparation, stipends, and meal tickets. The program ended after a confrontation between Panther students and an instructor critical of the Party.

chapters.title: Disability Access, Rent Strikes, Leaving the Party
chapters.summary: Howard describes Panther organizing of disabled wheelchair users that won curb cuts and ramps on county buildings, and rent strikes against landlords with lead-paint hazards. He attributes the end of the Party to Huey Newton's decision to dissolve all chapters and pull organizers to Oakland for the Bobby Seale mayoral campaign, leaving community programs stranded. Howard left the Party and never looked back.

chapters.title: Anonymity, FBI Surveillance, Coming Forward
chapters.summary: Howard hid his Panther background for years, working in retail management at Kmart and Service Merchandise across the South. FBI agents had earlier visited his elderly aunt in Tennessee. He kept the past from his daughter until she was a teenager. Eventually, at Billy X Jennings's urging, he attended a Washington-DC Panther reunion and began publicly reclaiming his history.

chapters.title: Sonoma County PACH and Continuing the Fight
chapters.summary: Reconnecting in California with his partner Carol, Howard discovered ongoing police killings by state troopers, county sheriffs, and local police in Sonoma County, alongside ICE immigration enforcement. He co-founded PACH, a 24-hour helpline and database for documenting police abuse. He cites the Sheriff's Deputy killing of 13-year-old Andy Lopez, who was carrying a toy weapon, as a case the system refused to indict.
